title: Financial Caregiving Basics
summary: Getting a handle on a parent's finances is one of the more sensitive parts of caregiving, and starting early makes it much easier.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
metaDescription: Getting a handle on a parent's finances is one of the more sensitive parts of caregiving, and starting early makes it much easier.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Understanding the full financial picture is the necessary first step before anything else.
Legal authority (like power of attorney) is separate from simply knowing account details.
Starting this conversation before a crisis gives everyone more options.

paragraphs: Before any decisions can be made, someone needs a reasonably complete picture: income sources, major accounts, debts, and recurring bills. This is often more involved than families expect, especially if a parent has managed finances independently for decades.

Simply knowing where accounts are is different from having legal authority to act on them. A financial power of attorney is generally required before a family member can make decisions or transactions on a parent's behalf — worth setting up well before it's urgently needed.

Starting this conversation while a parent is still fully able to participate gives the family far more options than waiting until a crisis forces the issue, when decisions often become more limited and more stressful.

nextSteps: Start gathering a full picture of accounts, bills, and debts.
Confirm whether a financial power of attorney already exists.
Have the conversation about future authority before it's urgently needed.
disclaimer: General information, not financial or legal advice — an elder law attorney or financial advisor can guide the specific steps for your family.
